coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional bone substitute using carbon dot and 3D printed calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite scaffolds for osteoclast inhibition and fluorescence imaging

coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Kyung Kwan
Raja, Naren
Yun, Hui-suk
Lee, Sang Cheon
Lee, Chang-Soo
coredata.dc:description:
Multifunctional bone substitute materials (BSM) have gained considerable attention with the exponential increase in aging populations. The development of hybrid materials for diagnosis and therapy of bone-related diseases and dysfunctions, especially, has been a significant challenge in the biological and the biomedical field, due to the shortage of agents with specificity and selectivity toward bone. In this study, a hybrid material, referred as Alen-CDs@CDHA, fabricated from alendronate-conjugated carbon dots (Alen-CDs) and calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite (CDHA, the mineral component of bones) scaffolds is offered as a novel multifunctional BSM for in vivo osteoclasts deactivation and fluorescence imaging. The fluorescent Alen-CDs were hydrothermally prepared using phytic acid as carbon source, followed by conjugating alendronate, for controlled alendronate release and fluorescent imaging under acidic conditions. As-prepared fluorescent Alen-CDs were consecutively immobilized on surfaces of CDHA scaffolds, exhibiting high affinity by bisphosphonate group, easily fabricated from α-tricalcium phosphate (α-TCP) paste using three-dimensional (3D) printing system. The resultant Alen-CDs@CDHA caused a significant decrease (> 50%) in viability of osteoclasts at 7 days after in vitro treatment. Furthermore, when Alen-CDs@CDHA was implanted in balb/c nude mice for in vivo evaluation, we found Alen-CDs@CDHA to be suitable for bone imaging through fluorescence signals, without necrosis or inflammatory symptoms in the epidermal tissues. Thus, these observations offer new opportunities for a novel and revolutionary use of Alen-CDs@CDHA as highly specific multifunctional BSM for bone diagnosis and imaging, and as bone-specific drug delivery materials, eventually providing anti-osteoclastogenic treatments solution for degenerative bone disorders.
Statement of significance Alen-CDs@CDHA significantly reduced the viability of osteoclasts and fluorescently imaged in vivo after transplantation, releasing drug via pH modulation. The development of fluorescence materials for bone imaging remains still a major challenge in the biomedical field owing to the shortage of selectivity and specificity. The results could lead to improvements in bone treatment strategies, as it could reduce the invasiveness of procedures and the associated negative outcomes, and increase the precision of strategies. Further, we believe that this study will be of interest to the readership of your journal as clearly focuses on the advancement of a biomaterial, where we have engineered a substance to substitute bone and integrate with a living system.

Finally, the authors acknowledge funding by Korea Institute of Planning and Evaluation for Technology in Food, Agriculture and Forestry (IPET) and Korea Smart Farm R&D Foundation (KosFarm) through Smart Farm Innovation Technology Development Program, funded by Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs (MAFRA) and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT), Rural Development Administration (RDA) (421042-04). The funding agencies had no role in study design; in the collection, analysis and interpretation of data; in the writing of the report; and in the decision to submit the article for publication.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of fluorescence imaging via Alen-CDs@CDHA implanted in balb/c nude mouse. (B) pH-controlled alendronate release mechanism from Alen-CDs, and alendronate metabolism pathway for osteoclast inhibition.

Structural characterizations between alendronate and Alen-CDs using (A) FT-IR, (B) 31P-NMR, and (C) XPS. (D) Zeta potential values of CDs and Alen-CDs. TEM images of the (E, a) morphology and (E, b) lattice fringes of Alen-CDs. (F) XRD spectra of CDHA and Alen-CDs CDHA. (G) SEM-EDS mapping analysis of CDHA and Alen-CDs@CDHA including calcium (Ca), phosphorus (P), oxygen (O), carbon (C), and nitrogen (N).

(A) Absorption spectrum, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra, (C) Concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity, (D) Photostability, (E) Quantum yield and (F) Fluorescence lifetime analysis of Alen-CDs. (G) Fluorescence images and (H) region of fluorescence intensity of Alen-CDs@CDHA according to the treated concentration of Alen-CDs.

(A) Schematic diagram of the 3D structure of combining Alen-CD with calcium ceramic scaffolds. (B) Fluorescence images of calcium ceramics and silica particles after being treated with Alen-CDs. (C) CDHA binding affinity of CDs and Alen-CDs. (D) Confocal microscopy images of zebrafish larvae treated with CDs, Alen-CDs, and Alizarin Red S (hyomandibula (hm), opercle (op), cleithrum (cl), branchiostegal ray1 (br1), branchiostegal ray2 (br2), and craniofacial (ch)). (E) Fluorescent images of balb/c nude mice injected with PBS, CDs, and Alen-CDs, and (F) fluorescent images of legs dissected from balb/c nude mice injected with PBS, CDs, and Alen-CDs.

. (A) Schematic representation of drug release from Alen-CDs under acidic pH conditions. (B) Time-dependent 31P-NMR spectrum of Alen-CDs at pH 7.4 and pH 5.4. (C) Alendronate release profiles of Alen-CDs with pH and time variations (n = 3). (D) Fluorescence images of Alen-CDs@CDHA with pH and time variations, and (E) their region of fluorescence values.

(A) Fluorescence images of osteoclasts cultured with CDHA using confocal microscopy. (B) SEM image of CDHA cultured with osteoclast cells. (C) SEM-EDS elemental mapping image of CDHA cultured with osteoclast cells including carbon (C), nitrogen (N), calcium (Ca), phosphorus (P), and oxygen (O). (D and E) Anti-osteoclastogenic effects of CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA at different treated concentrations of CDs and Alen-CDs for 7 days (n = 3, *p < 0.05). (F) Fluorescence images of CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA implanted balb/c nude mice, and (G) their fluorescence intensity values. (H) H & E-stained images of epithelial tissues of balb/c nude mice after implantation with CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA for 24 h.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional bone substitute materials (BSM) have gained considerable attention with the exponential increase in aging populations. The development of hybrid materials for diagnosis and therapy of bone-related diseases and dysfunctions, especially, has been a significant challenge in the biological and the biomedical field, due to the shortage of agents with specificity and selectivity toward bone. In this study, a hybrid material, referred as Alen-CDs@CDHA, fabricated from alendronate-conjugated carbon dots (Alen-CDs) and calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite (CDHA, the mineral component of bones) scaffolds is offered as a novel multifunctional BSM for in vivo osteoclasts deactivation and fluorescence imaging. The fluorescent Alen-CDs were hydrothermally prepared using phytic acid as carbon source, followed by conjugating alendronate, for controlled alendronate release and fluorescent imaging under acidic conditions. As-prepared fluorescent Alen-CDs were consecutively immobilized on surfaces of CDHA scaffolds, exhibiting high affinity by bisphosphonate group, easily fabricated from α-tricalcium phosphate (α-TCP) paste using three-dimensional (3D) printing system. The resultant Alen-CDs@CDHA caused a significant decrease (> 50%) in viability of osteoclasts at 7 days after in vitro treatment. Furthermore, when Alen-CDs@CDHA was implanted in balb/c nude mice for in vivo evaluation, we found Alen-CDs@CDHA to be suitable for bone imaging through fluorescence signals, without necrosis or inflammatory symptoms in the epidermal tissues. Thus, these observations offer new opportunities for a novel and revolutionary use of Alen-CDs@CDHA as highly specific multifunctional BSM for bone diagnosis and imaging, and as bone-specific drug delivery materials, eventually providing anti-osteoclastogenic treatments solution for degenerative bone disorders.

Alen-CDs@CDHA significantly reduced the viability of osteoclasts and fluorescently imaged in vivo after transplantation, releasing drug via pH modulation. The development of fluorescence materials for bone imaging remains still a major challenge in the biomedical field owing to the shortage of selectivity and specificity. The results could lead to improvements in bone treatment strategies, as it could reduce the invasiveness of procedures and the associated negative outcomes, and increase the precision of strategies. Further, we believe that this study will be of interest to the readership of your journal as clearly focuses on the advancement of a biomaterial, where we have engineered a substance to substitute bone and integrate with a living system.

ce:section.ce:para: Bone is a rigid tissue in vertebrate animals that forms the skeleton which protects organs, enables mobility, and stores minerals [1–3]. However, the prevalence of bone-related disorders caused by stress and unregulated dietary habits is quite increasing, and this is causing serious problems in our aging populations. Consequently, various artificial bone substitute materials (BSM) for the efficient treatment of bone disorders have been widely developed, drawing them a considerable attention to clinical applications such as oncologic surgery, traumatology, and spine surgery [4,5]. For BSM to be suitable for the replacement or regeneration of bone, they are generally required to be biocompatible and mechanically stable, exhibiting no adverse inflammatory responses [6]. Thus, BSMs based on calcium phosphates (CaP), ceramics, and polymers have previously been considered for the replacement of extensive local bone loss or the creation of customized prosthesis to match the bone defect site [7,8]. Compared to traditional bioceramics, such as metals, polymers, and ceramics, CaP offers certain advantages such as osteoconductivity, consistency, low cost, and biocompatibility [9,10]. Thus, the advanced BSM based on CaP have been widely used in clinical applications as bone replacement materials with high mechanical strength, large surface areas, and tunable surface functionalities [11–13].

Over the last few decades, numerous attempts have been made to provide detection or imaging techniques for identification and treatment of bone diseases at the early stage. A continuous monitoring over bone therapy has especially been a crucial approach for the successful healing of bone disorders after bone graft surgery. X-ray and computed tomography (CT) are the most frequently used bone imaging techniques; however, both require that a part of the body be exposed to potentially detrimental ionizing radiation [14]. Although recent advances in fluorescence instrumentation provide alternatives to traditional bone imaging methods, the development of fluorescence materials for in vivo bone imaging is still a major challenge in the biomedical field owing to a shortage of selective and specific bone imaging agents.

Carbon dots (CDs), which are carbon-based nanomaterials with tens of nanometers in size, have been widely challenged in the biomedical field because of their unique characteristics, such as remarkable biocompatibility, water-solubility, photostability, and abundant low-cost sources, when compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [15–19]. Some of CDs are often available for in vivo imaging due to their optical properties such as long-wavelength excitation and/or up-conversion photoluminescence emission. Recently, the in vitro cellular imaging of CDs was investigated, and the result further demonstrated the feasibility of CDs for in vivo tissue imaging in zebrafish and mice [20–25]. Besides, tissue engineering using CDs has been explored to improve the osteoinductive, physicochemical, and biomimetic properties [26,27].

In this study, we describe the preparation of a novel hybrid BSM (Alen-CDs@CDHA) consisted of alendronate-conjugated CDs (Alen-CDs) and calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite (CDHA) scaffolds with abilities of osteoclasts deactivation and fluorescence imaging. CDHA scaffolds, which are similar to the component segments of the bone, were easily fabricated from α-tricalcium phosphate (α-TCP) paste using three-dimensional (3D) printing system. Alen-CDs were hydrothermally prepared using phytic acid as a carbon precursor, covalently followed by conjugation with alendronate forming the phosphoramidate bond. Strong fluorescence of Alen-CDs can be beneficial to in vivo monitoring of bones, due to high affinity of Alen-CDs to calcium ions such as calcium-based ceramics. The CDHA scaffolds adsorbed with Alen-CDs (Alen-CDs@CDHA) resulted in a multifunctional BSM that could be used to monitor drug release in bone disorder treatments. Alen-CDs@CDHA were also confirmed to emit fluorescence signals in balb/c nude mice after implantation (Fig. 1A). Further, the phosphoramidate bond in the Alen-CDs allows release of alendronate under acidic condition on bone resorption, resulting in the apoptosis of osteoclast cells by interfering with the specific metabolism (Fig. 1B). Finally, we suggest that Alen-CDs@CDHA can be utilized as a progressive BSM with theranostic functions for the treatment of bone disorders.

Phytic acid 75% (w/w), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.5% purity), imidazole, 1α, 25-dihydroxyvitamin D3, ethyl(dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), and Alizarin Red S were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA) and used without further purification. A hydrothermal synthesis reactor was purchased from INexus (Seoul, South Korea). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Dojindo Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan), and the tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase and alkaline phosphatase double-stain kit was purchased from Takara Bio Inc. (Shiga, Japan). Alendronate sodium trihydrate was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Deuterated solvents were purchased from Cambridge Isotope Laboratories and used without modification. Penicillin/streptomycin (P/S), phosphate buffer saline (PBS), alpha minimum essential medium (α-MEM), Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), recombinant human receptor activator of nuclear factor-kappa Β ligand (RANKL), and phalloidin staining dye (Alexa Fluor 488) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Waltham, MA, USA). A cell scraper for the subcultures was purchased from SPL Life Sciences (Gyeonggi-do, South Korea). RAW 264.7 and MC3T3-E1 cells were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). Cell fixation and permeabilization solution (Cytofix/Cytoperm) were purchased from BD Biosciences.

Structure and optical analysis were tested by using fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Bruker Corp., Billerica, MA, USA), 31phosphorus-nuclear magnetic resonance (31P-NMR) (Bruker BioSpin, Avance III, 600 MHz), mass spectrometer (AB SCIEX, TripleTOF 5600), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA), Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern Panalytical, Zetasizer nano zs, Malvern, UK), Zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Panalytical, Zetasizer nano zs, Malvern, UK), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN, 300 kV), X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku, MiniFlex, Tokyo, Japan), scanning electron microscope (SEM) (ZEISS Sigma, Oberkochen, Germany), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Bruker Quantax, MA, USA), digital fluorescence imaging system (LAS 4000, ImageQuantTM, GE Healthcare, IL, USA), microplate reader (SpectraMax M2, Molecular Devices), fluorescence microscope (EVOS M5000, Thermo Fisher, USA), confocal microscopy (FV1000, Olympus), and IVIS Lumina II in vivo imaging system (Perkin Elmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, Alen-CDs, and Alen-CDs@CDHA
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs were prepared with phytic acid and ethylenediamine using a facile hydrothermal method. Briefly, 6 mL 75% (w/w) phytic acid and 2 mL ethylenediamine were carefully dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. This solution was then transferred to a hydrothermal synthesis reactor (Teflon-autoclave, 50 mL). The Teflon-autoclave was plated on a drying oven (Faithful Inst. Co., Ltd, China) at 200 °C for 12 h. After the polymerization reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The obtained mixtures were purified sequentially using a 0.2-mm syringe filter and dialyzed with deionized water (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) for 3 days to remove excessive impurity. For the preparation of Alen-CDs, 20 mg EDC and 200 µL imidazole (0.1 M, pH 6.0) were dissolved in the prepared CD solution (5 mL, 1 mg/mL), and the solution was then incubated at 50°C for 3 h. Thereafter, 10 mg alendronate sodium trihydrate was added to the solution of imidazole functionalized CDs, followed by stirring for 6 h. The resultant clear solution was lyophilized via dialysis for 2 days and a deep-brown-colored powder was finally collected for characterization. The Alen-CDs@CDHA were prepared by immersing CDHA in the Alen-CD solution (400 µg/mL) for 12 h at room temperature. The resulting deep brown-colored Alen-CDs@CDHA were washed with distilled water to remove nonspecifically bound Alen-CDs, followed by drying for 2 days in the dark at room temperature.

The CDHA scaffolds mentioned above were fabricated as follows. An α-tricalcium phosphate (α-TCP) paste with proper rheological characteristics for stacking a stable three-dimensional (3D) structure through a 3D printing system was formulated by mixing the ground powder with a 1 wt% solution of hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (prepared in 30% ethanol). The powder to liquid ratio was set to 1.67 for effective extrusion. α-TCP scaffolds were fabricated using a paste extruding deposition system, which is a representative 3D printing system, and they were dried at 37 °C for 24 h prior to cementation. The pure CDHA scaffolds (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm × 0.2 cm) were obtained by hydrolyzing α-TCP by immersing it in PBS for 24 h, and were then washed with deionized water several times and dried completely at room temperature for 3 days prior to further experimentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the Alen-CDs was determined using the comparative method described by Williams et al. [28], which is a well-known slope method. Anthracene (QY 27%) was employed as a standard fluorophore. The fluorescence QY of Alen-CDs was determined using anthracene (ΦF = 0.27 in ethanol) as a fluorescent standard. The QY was calculated using the following equation.
Φ

Where ΦF is the fluorescence QY, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, F is the area under the corrected emission curve, and n is the refractive index of the solvents used. The subscripts S and X refer to the standard and the unknown, respectively.

Controlled release of the alendronate from the Alen-CDs or Alen-CDs@CDHA was investigated under different pH conditions (pH 7.4 and pH 5.4) using 31P-NMR spectroscopy and a mass spectrometer. To prepare samples for 31P-NMR analysis, Alen-CDs (500 µg/mL) were transferred into a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) and dialyzed in acetate buffer (pH 5.4) and Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4), respectively. Each dialyzed solution was collected at predetermined time intervals (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, and 12 h) for 31P-NMR analysis. For the mass spectrometer analysis, Alen-CDs@CDHA scaffolds were transferred into each 24-well plate containing 1 mL acetate buffer (0.1 M) and 1 mL Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M), respectively. Thereafter, the supernatant of each sample was collected at 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, 5 h, 8 h, and 12 h. Each supernatant was continually recovered and renewed with an equal volume of fresh buffer, followed by analysis using a mass spectrometer.

As pre-osteoclasts, RAW 264.7 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% P/S at 37 ℃. When the RAW 264.7 cells reached 85% confluence, they were passaged using a cell scraper. The sterilized CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA (0.5 × 0.5 cm) were immersed in the cell culture medium (supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% P/S) in 24-well plates for mineralization, respectively. After 24 h, the RAW 264.7 cells were seeded onto scaffolds at densities of 1.0 × 106 cells/cm2, followed by incubation in a humidified incubator under 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The medium was exchanged every 3 days and washed twice with PBS for the MTT assay after 7 days.

CDHA scaffolds (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm) were used for the tissue engineering in vitro studies. For sterilization, the prepared CDHA scaffolds were pre-immersed in 70% ethanol with UV light (365 nm) for 1 h. After sterilization, a total of 1 × 106 RAW 264.7 cells were directly seeded onto the CDHA scaffold and then submerged in the complete medium in a 48-well plate. The cell medium was changed every 3 days and cultured in a humidified incubator under 5% CO2 at 37 ℃.

D3 and RANKL were used for the differentiation of osteoclasts from RAW 264.7 cells. In brief, the RAW 264.7 cells were cultured onto a sterilized scaffold (0.5 × 0.5 cm) at a density of 1.0 × 106 cells/scaffold in a 24-well plate with a humidified incubator. After 3 days of incubation, the culture medium was replaced with an α-MEM solution with D3 (20 nM) and RANKL (100 ng/mL) to differentiate the pre-osteoclast cells. Cultures were maintained for 14 days, changing the differentiation medium and washing with PBS every 3 days.

To confirm the anti-osteoclastogenic effect, CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA were prepared for 7 days. Subsequently, the CCK-8 solution (100 µL) was added to each 24-well plate with the cell-cultured scaffold. After 30 min of incubation, the supernatant (10 µL) was transferred to a 96-well plate and the absorbance measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader. Likewise, cytotoxicity tests for the Alen-CDs were conducted on MC3T3-E1 cells. Briefly, MC3T3-E1 cells were cultured onto a flat-bottomed 96-well plate at a density of 1.0 × 104 cells/well in a humidified incubator under 5% CO2 at 37°C. After incubating for 24 h, the alpha-MEM in each well was decanted and the cells were washed twice with PBS and treated with various concentrations (50, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 µg/mL) of Alen-CDs for 24 h. Subsequently, wells with Alen-CD treated cells were washed with PBS and 10 µL of the CCK-8 solution added. After 30 min of incubation, the absorbance of each well was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader. All experiments were performed in triplicate, and relative cell viability (%) was expressed as a percentage relative to the viability of the untreated control cells.

The cells cultured on bare CDHA and Alen-CDs@CDHA were washed with an 1 × PBS buffer and fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution for 30 min. The cells fixed on each scaffold were then dehydrated using a graded ethanol method [29]. Briefly, the cells fixed on the scaffold followed by glutaraldehyde were submerged in 25%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 90%, and 100% ethanol solutions for 15 min each. Finally, the dehydrated samples were dried in a desiccator for 12 h before SEM analysis.

To detect tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase (TRACP) from the osteoclasts, a TRACP & alkaline phosphatase (ALP) double-stain kit was used as follows. RAW 264.7 cells were cultured onto a flat-bottomed 96-well plate at a density of 1.0 × 104 cells/well with an α-MEM solution with D3 (20 nM) and RANKL (100 ng/mL) in a humidified incubator under 5% CO2 at 37 °C. After 7 days of incubation, the supernatant of the cultured medium was washed with sterile PBS and added to 50 µL of a fixation solution at room temperature for 5 min. The substrate solution was added to each well and incubated at 37 ℃ for 30 min for the activity staining of the osteoclast. After incubation, the substrate solution was removed and washed with sterile distilled water to stop the reaction. Finally, each stained sample was examined using a fluorescence microscope.

For histological analysis, the hematoxylin and eosin (H & E) stain was used as follows. First, the fixed organs were trimmed to a thickness of approximately 2–3 mm in a shape suitable for tissue specimen production after the extracted organs were fixed in neutral buffered formalin. The trimmed specimens were then placed in a cassette and subjected to tissue treatment for 13 h. The treated tissues were then cut to a thickness of approximately 3–4 µm using a cutter, attached to a slide, and then dried. The dried specimens were washed with distilled water after the deparaffinization and rehydration processes. Finally, the H & E staining was carried out following the standard method [30].

Zebrafish embryos were obtained by natural spawning of an adult AB line. Wild-type adult zebrafish (AB line) were maintained by following the standard procedures (www.zfin.org) of a 14 h light/10 h dark cycle at 28 °C. Before virus injections, the fish were anesthetized in tricaine solution and lined up on a 1.5% agarose gel plate in a Petri dish. Around 3 nL of Alen-CDs (15 µg/µL) was injected into the abdominal cavity of the zebrafish. Alizarin Red S was employed as a standard bone staining dye. As a positive control, 3 nL of Alizarin Red S (300 ng/µL) was injected into the abdominal cavity of the zebrafish using the same method as for the Alen-CDs. Then, the injected larvae were transferred to plates with E3 egg water and reared at 28.5 °C. After 2 h of injection, fluorescence images of live specimens were acquired at a 405nm wavelength using confocal microscopy.

Balb/c nude mice (4 weeks, female) were obtained from NARA biotech (Seoul, Korea) and cared for according to the guidelines provided by KRIBB. To study in vivo fluorescence imaging with the scaffolds, CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA were implanted under the skin of 6-week-old balb/c nude mice (20–25 g) for in vivo imaging. The implanted CDHA scaffolds were well tolerated in all mice and the fluorescence images were obtained from an IVIS Lumina II in vivo imaging system. The balb/c nude mice used for ex vivo and in vivo imaging were anesthetized with isoflurane using a gas anesthesia system. All of the experimental animal care and handling procedures were carried out in accordance with the guidelines from KRIBB, and all of the experimental protocols were approved by KRIBB-IACUC (approval number: KRIBB-AEC-18128).

A t-test and analysis of variance (ANOVA) were used to determine the statistical significance of differences between control and experimental groups. All p values ​​are marked with an asterisk in the figures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, Alen-CDs, and Alen-CDs@CDHA
ce:section.ce:para: CDs, Alen-CDs, and Alen-CDs@CDHA were sequentially prepared according to the processes shown in Fig. S1. Both the prepared CDs and Alen-CDs have a size of about 10 nm (Fig. S2). The chemical composition and interfacial interactions of the Alen-CDs were investigated using FT-IR. Broad peaks for the Alen-CDs were observed at 1021.8 cm−1 (v s) and 970.4 cm−1 (v as), and attributed to the stretching region of the bisphosphonate, indicating that the surface of the Alen-CDs was exposed to the bisphosphonate group (Fig. 2 A) [31–33]. The 31P-NMR provided structural analysis of the hybridization state of the phosphorus, representing the proton decoupled 31P-NMR spectra of the alendronate, CDs, and Alen-CDs, and was crucial for differentiating between mono-, di-, and tri-phosphoesters using characteristic chemical shifts. Both alendronate and Alen-CDs showed a peak assigned to the phosphorous atom of the bisphosphonate group at 17.8 ppm, whereas the CDs exhibited two peaks at 0.42 ppm and 101.83 ppm, corresponding to a phosphorous atom of the phosphonate group (Fig. 2B and Fig. S3a) [34,35]. Moreover, a new peak that appeared at 82.24 ppm was considered to arise from a phosphoramidate bond formed between alendronate and CDs, indicating that alendronate molecules were successfully conjugated to CDs via the phosphoramidate bond (Fig. S3b).

In the XPS profiles, alendronate represented a single peak for P–O and P=O at 133.3 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of bisphosphonate. However, the Alen-CD peaks were at 133.4 eV and 134.6 eV, and indicated the presence of a new P–N binding energy as well as the P–O and P=O binding energy, respectively (Fig. 2C) [36]. The appearance of the P–N bond represents a covalent anchoring of the alendronate onto the CDs surface, which implies that a phosphoramidate bond [O=P(OH)(NH)-] was formed between the amine and phosphate groups. The overall XPS survey peaks in alendronate and Alen-CDs are displayed in Fig. S4.

The physical properties of the CDs and Alen-CDs were detected using a zeta potential analyzer. The zeta potential values between the CDs and Alen-CDs were recorded as 5.7 mV and −2.6 mV, respectively, indicating that the surface of the Alen-CDs was changed to a bisphosphonate group (Fig. 2D). TEM confirmed that the morphology of the CDs and Alen-CDs involved the crystalline and amorphous structures, which were made up of sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms at the edges, or defected sites as amorphous structures (Fig. 2E, a and Fig. S5). Furthermore, the Alen-CDs showed independent nanoparticles to be approximately 10 nm in diameter, which involved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.213 nm, close to the (020) and (100) diffraction facets of the graphite carbon (Fig. 2E, b).

Further structural characterization of CDHA and Alen-CDs@CDHA were conducted using XRD, SEM, and SEM-EDS. The CDHA displayed low crystallinity (PDF: 46-0905; Fig. 2F) [37]. In contrast, a broad peak centered at approximately 20° (2θ) indicated an interplanar spacing of the conventional CDs in the XRD pattern of the Alen-CDs@CDHA, demonstrating the presence of Alen-CDs bound to the CDHA [38,39]. The atomic composition of the Alen-CDs@CDHA was determined using elemental mapping with SEM-EDS. The CDHA is mostly composed of calcium (Ca), phosphorus (P), and oxygen (O), whereas carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) were attributed to the Alen-CDs and observed at Alen-CDs@CDHA (Fig. 2G). The detailed atomic compositions and weight ratios between CDHA and Alen-CDs@CDHA are summarized in Table S1.

CDs have special excitation-dependent fluorescence emission properties, which result from the surface state of the CDs [40,41]. These optical characteristics of the CDs occurred when the surface was either trapped by passivation agents or modified with functional groups that could donate or withdraw electrons [42,43]. Thus, phosphorus and amino groups could be effective candidates for achieving controlled surface passivation with changes in the fluorescence properties of the CDs [44–46]. Based on these principles, the Alen-CDs were found to have different optical characteristics from the CDs. In the UV-visible spectra, the CDs and Alen-CDs exhibited two absorption bands characterizing the electron transition state of the surface, respectively. The UV-visible spectra of the CDs and Alen-CDs were similar in the ultraviolet region attributed to the π–π* electron transition state of the aromatic sp2 domains from the aromatic core (Fig. 3 A and Fig. S6a). However, the absorption spectrum of Alen-CDs in the visible region, which contributes to the n–π* electron transition state of the surface, is clearly distinguished from the CDs owing to surface modifications by the alendronate.

For further examination of the optical properties of the CDs and Alen-CDs, the emission activity according to the excitation wavelengths was investigated using a fluorescence spectroscopic study. Compared to the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs, the Alen-CDs exhibited a noticeable excitation-dependent behavior and redshifted to longer wavelengths owing to the surface state tuned by the alendronate (Fig. 3B and Fig. S6b).

The quantitative fluorescence intensity signals of Alen-CDs with gradually increased concentrations did not show any fluorescence self-quenching behavior (Fig. 3C and Fig. S7). For the CDs and Alen-CDs, there was a negligible reduction in fluorescence emission intensity observed after 1 h of continuous irradiation at 440 nm, using a 150-W steady-state xenon lamp, which indicated that they had excellent photostability (Fig. 3D and Fig. S8). The fluorescence lifetime and QY of the Alen-CDs were determined to be 3.75 ns, and 12% at room temperature, respectively (Fig. 3E and 3F). The QY calculation of the reference fluorophore and CDs were represented in Fig. S9.

To investigate the solid-state fluorescence properties of the Alen-CDs absorbed by the CDHA, the fluorescence emission of Alen-CDs@CDHA was measured using a digital fluorescence imaging system. As the treatment concentrations of the Alen-CDs increased, the fluorescence emission signals of the Alen-CDs@CDHA were quantitatively increased and saturated at a concentration of 400 µg/mL of Alen-CDs ( Fig. 3G and Fig. 3H). This result served as a prerequisite to manufacturing the optimum Alen-CDs@CDHA.

Bisphosphonate is classified as a functional group with a strong affinity for calcium ions [47]. Consequently, Alen-CDs have a strong adsorption affinity to various calcium ceramics, which are candidates for BSM via chelation with calcium ions (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4B suggests that Alen-CDs have a high affinity for diverse BSM containing hydroxyapatite (HA), β-tricalcium phosphate (β-TCP), α-tricalcium phosphate (α-TCP), and CDHA. Therefore, the strong fluorescence attributed to specific calcium-binding of the Alen-CDs was observed for all the calcium phosphate-based materials except for the silica particles. CDs showed an extremely lower affinity for CDHA than Alen-CDs (Fig. 4C).

Further investigations into the potential bone affinity of Alen-CDs were performed using live zebrafish larvae and balb/c nude mice. The fluorescence bone images of live zebrafish larvae after the treatment of the CDs and Alen-CDs are shown in Fig. 4D. Alizarin Red S was used as a positive control to identify mineralized bones. Two hours after the post-abdominal injection (2 hpi), the Alizarin Red S and Alen-CD-treated fluorescence images perfectly corresponded in the head skeleton of the zebrafish larvae with high specificity, indicating a strong interaction between the Alen-CD and bone tissue. In contrast, no fluorescence signal was generated in the zebrafish larvae injected with CDs.

Furthermore, balb/c nude mice (6-weeks-old) were used to demonstrate the potential efficacy of the Alen-CDs. Time-dependent whole-body fluorescence images using an IVIS Lumina II in vivo imaging system were measured after tail vein injections of Alen-CDs at a dose of 100 µL in physiological saline (10 mg/kg). The whole-body fluorescence images of the balb/c nude mice were observed at predetermined time intervals after tail vein injection of the Alen-CDs (Fig. S10). There was a significantly higher fluorescence signal 24 h after injection in the legs and tails of the Alen-CD-injected balb/c nude mice when compared with those for the CDs (Fig. 4E). To investigate this further, the legs of the mice injected with CDs and Alen-CDs were dissected. The fluorescence intensity of the legs of the mice was quantitatively assessed for comparison (Fig. 4F), and was found to be significantly high in those injected with Alen-CDs when compared with those for the PBS and CDs. These results suggest that the Alen-CDs were immobilized on the bone tissue, indicating that they could retain their fluorescence and drug release ability in vivo. Motivated by the outstanding in vivo efficacy of the Alen-CDs, a biodistribution test was performed in the major organs (Fig. S11). The fluorescence signals in the major organs injected with Alen-CDs were relatively lower when compared with those of the CDs, suggesting that Alen-CDs were efficiently delivered to the bone tissues. The biocompatibility of Alen-CDs was evaluated using H & E staining, and it was confirmed that the Alen-CDs had superior biocompatibility without any inflammation and toxicity in the major organs (Fig. S12).

The controlled release of alendronate from the Alen-CDs under acidic conditions is shown in Fig. 5 A. The phosphorus–nitrogen (P–N) bond in the phosphoramidate group, a class of phosphorus compounds structurally related to phosphates via the substitution of a P–OH for a P–NH-, is known to be hydrolytically unstable under acidic conditions suitable for osteoclast suppression [48–50]. Accordingly, a controllable release of alendronate from the Alen-CDs was observed through the cleavage of the phosphoramidate bond at pH 5.4, but this did not occur at the physiological pH of 7.4. To estimate the behavior of alendronate release, the pH was intentionally adjusted using a Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1 M) and acetate buffer (pH 5.4, 0.1 M). The changes in the 31P-NMR spectra of the Alen-CDs with different times and pH values are shown in Fig. 5B. Small changes in two peaks (0.43 and 17.8 ppm) corresponding to phosphate and bisphosphonate on the surface of Alen-CDs were discovered at pH 7.4, whereas larger changes in both peaks were observed at pH 5.4. In particular, the clear decrease in the 17.8 ppm peak at pH 5.4 provides explicit evidence that the bisphosphonate is removed from the Alen-CDs. The determination of the mass of alendronate by mass spectrometry under pH 7.4 and pH 5.4, which provides direct evidence of alendronate release from Alen-CDs under acidic conditions, is shown in Fig. 5C. When compared to pH 7.4, the amount of released alendronate reached approximately 70% at pH 5.4 after 12 h.

To confirm the controlled drug release behavior of Alen-CDs@CDHA, they were immersed in pH 7.4 and pH 5.4 buffers, respectively. The Alen-CDs were removed from the CDHA owing to the hydrolysis of the phosphoramidate bond at pH 5.4, resulting in a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of Alen- Alen-CDs@CDHA over time (Fig. 5D). The fluorescence intensity in the pH 5.4 buffer, however, was gradually increased over time for the immersed Alen-CDs@CDHA (Fig. S13). The change in fluorescence intensity of the Alen-CDs@CDHA was significantly reduced to less than 50% at pH 5.4, whereas a slight decrease was observed at pH 7.4, demonstrating the pH-responsive drug release ability of Alen-CDs@CDHA (Fig. 5E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-osteoclastogenic effect and in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Alendronate has been used extensively as a bone-targeted drug owing to its therapeutic effects in various bone diseases and its highly efficient in vivo binding to bone tissues [51,52]. The systemic application of alendronate-conjugated nanomaterials could be used for the treatment of osteoporosis [53,54] or the coating of anticancer drugs for targeted bone metastatic cancer treatments [55]. Before verifying the anti-osteoclastogenic effects of Alen-CDs, toxicity and cellular imaging of the Alen-CDs were estimated using MC3T3-E1 cells that were not affected by alendronate (Fig. S14). Tissue engineering using CDHA with RAW 264.7 cells was then conducted to establish the anti-osteoclastogenic effects of Alen-CDs@CDHA. A confocal microscopy image of RAW 264.7 cells cultured onto CDHA scaffold for 3 days after phalloidin staining, which shows that the cells were well distributed on the CDHA scaffold, is presented in Fig. 6 A. Moreover, the RAW 264.7 cells cultured in the CDHA scaffold exhibited equivalent viability when compared to the control cells, which were cultured in a 24-well plate (Fig. S15). SEM images of the CDHA scaffold surfaces provided conclusive evidence that the RAW 264.7 cells were well cultured on the CDHA scaffold (Fig. 6B). These results are consistent with the findings of the SEM-EDS analysis. Compared to the bare CDHA scaffold, the CDHA cultured with RAW 264.7 cells appeared to show organic peaks such as C and N atoms by SEM-EDS (Fig. 6C and Fig. S16).

To assure the anti-osteoclastogenic effect of Alen-CDs@CDHA, RAW 264.7 cells were differentiated to osteoclasts cells with Alen-CDs@CDHA using a culture medium containing RANKL and D3. The Alen-CDs@CDHA prepared from the CDHA treated with various concentrations of Alen-CDs exhibited an excellent anti-osteoclastogenic performance, suppressing more than 40% of the osteoclasts over 7 days (Fig. 6D and 6E). To visualize the anti-osteoclastogenic effect of Alen-CDs, TRACP staining was carried out, which allowed osteoclast suppression by Alen-CDs to be observed (Fig. S17). Apart from the anti-osteoclastogenic effect, to establish the potential monitoring efficacy of Alen-CDs@CDHA, CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA were implanted under the epidermal tissues of the balb/c nude mice (Fig. S18). After implantation for 24 h, fluorescence images of each mouse were obtained using an IVIS Lumina II in vivo imaging system. A high fluorescence signal was observed at Alen-CDs@CDHA, whereas the fluorescence signal of mice implanted with CDHA and CDs@CDHA was not found (Fig. 6F and 6G). Finally, the tissue toxicity to CDHA, CDs@CDHA, and Alen-CDs@CDHA was assessed using H & E staining for the epithelial tissues on each scaffold (Fig. 6H). Necrosis, inflammatory symptoms, and tissue destruction were not found in the H&E-stained epithelial tissues. These results indicated that the Alen-CDs@CDHA could be readily used as a functional BSM with anti-osteoclastogenic effects, and the implantation site could be monitored with fluorescence signals.

The present study describes the preparation of an Alen-CD- and CDHA hybrid Alen-CDs@CDHA scaffold using a simplistic method for application as a multifunctional BSM with anti-osteoclastogenic effects and fluorescence monitoring at the implantation site. The highly biocompatible CDHA can be used as a BSM and was easily manufactured with a 3D printing system. The Ale-CDs, which were adsorbed onto the CDHA scaffold, were synthesized hydrothermally and enabled controlled drug release by pH and monitoring at the implant site using fluorescence signals. In particular, the Alen-CDs@CDHA significantly decreased the viability of osteoclasts by more than 50% over 7 days in vitro. Moreover, when the Alen-CDs@CDHA were implanted into balb/c nude mice, they could be monitored using the fluorescence signals without necrosis or inflammatory symptoms in the epidermal tissues. However, the effects of the Alen-CDs@CDHA on bone repair in vivo have not yet been studied. Nevertheless, we expect that this BSM, with its theranostic capabilities, could potentially replace the use of autografts and allografts in bone disorder treatments in the future.
